front: Figure: eldest son of Pepin the Short, who deposed the last Merovingian king Childeric III in 750
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: namesake of the Carolingian dynasty, which succeeded the Merovingian dynasty
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: known by the Latinized name Carolus Magnus, or "Charles the Great"
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Figure: Alessandro Barbero's biography attributes his "genocidal tendencies" to a desire to emulate the kings of Israel
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Event: crowned "Emperor of the Romans" by Pope Leo III at Christmas Mass, December 25, 800, at Old St. Peter's Basilica
work: Coronation as Holy Roman Emperor (800 CE)

front: Event: made Charlemagne the first ruler to hold the title of (Western Roman) Emperor since the fall of Rome
work: Coronation as Holy Roman Emperor (800 CE)

front: Event: modern historians like Henry Mayr-Harting argue this was planned in advance at Paderborn (after a man named Winigis was saved from mutilation), contradicting Einhard's claim it took a humble Charlemagne by surprise
work: Coronation as Holy Roman Emperor (800 CE)

front: Event: justified in the Annals of Lorsch partly by citing the gender of the reigning Byzantine empress Irene of Athens and the concept of translatio imperii
work: Coronation as Holy Roman Emperor (800 CE)

front: Event: examined, along with the women around Charlemagne, in Janet Nelson's biography King and Emperor
work: Coronation as Holy Roman Emperor (800 CE)

front: War: a roughly 33-year war (beginning 772) to conquer and forcibly convert the pagan Saxons, fought simultaneously with Charlemagne's Spanish campaign
work: The Saxon Wars

front: War: Charlemagne destroyed the Saxons' sacred pillar Irminsul during this conflict, per the Royal Frankish Annals
work: The Saxon Wars

front: Event: some 4,500 Saxons were executed in a single day here, per Einhard
work: The Saxon Wars

front: Figure: pagan Saxon chieftain who led an 8-year revolt against Charlemagne before being captured and forced to convert, later becoming a symbol of German independence
work: The Saxon Wars

front: Event: at this capitulation, Charlemagne forced the Saxons to convert to Christianity on pain of death for practicing pagan rituals
work: The Saxon Wars

front: Figure: repeatedly urged Charlemagne to stop massacring the Saxons and make peace instead
work: The Saxon Wars

front: War: Germanic people, neighbors of the Saxons, whose revolts Charlemagne put down after they had earlier martyred the missionary St. Boniface
work: The Saxon Wars

front: Figure: last king of the Lombards, whose daughter Desiderata married Charlemagne; defeated and deposed after threatening papal lands in Italy
work: The Lombard Campaign

front: Place: Charlemagne defeated the Lombards at this city, their capital, ancient Ticinum, per his biographer Einhard
work: The Lombard Campaign

front: Work: this history of the Lombards was written by Paul the Deacon under Charlemagne's sponsorship, after the kingdom's conquest
work: The Lombard Campaign

front: Figure: Muslim governor of Barcelona whose alliance with Charlemagne, sealed at Paderborn, saw Charlemagne welcomed into Barcelona and Girona
work: The Iberian Campaign & the Battle of Roncevaux Pass

front: Place: after an unsuccessful siege of this city, Charlemagne's returning army was ambushed, leading him to destroy Pamplona in revenge
work: The Iberian Campaign & the Battle of Roncevaux Pass

front: Battle: Basque forces ambushed and destroyed Charlemagne's rearguard here in 778, killing Eggihard, Mayor of the Palace — a defeat later immortalized as the Song of Roland
work: The Iberian Campaign & the Battle of Roncevaux Pass

front: Place: Charlemagne established this military buffer zone south of Septimania, later relinquished by the Treaty of Corbeil
work: The Iberian Campaign & the Battle of Roncevaux Pass

front: Figure: Charlemagne's son and eventual heir, who later captured Barcelona, cementing the Spanish March, per Notker the Stammerer
work: The Iberian Campaign & the Battle of Roncevaux Pass

front: Figure: scholar summoned from England to run the palace school at Aachen; Einhard called him "the most learned man anywhere to be found"
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this collection of math riddles by Alcuin of York, including four river-crossing puzzles, was written for Charlemagne's court
back: Propositiones ad Acuendos Juvenes (Alcuin of York)
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this biography of Charlemagne was written in imitation of Suetonius's The Twelve Caesars
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this biography portrays Charlemagne's mother Bertrada favorably
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this biography portrays Charlemagne's third wife Fastrada as a cruel queen
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Figure: this biographer of Charlemagne supposedly eloped to Seligenstadt with Charlemagne's daughter Emma
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: written by Theodulf of Orleans as a confused response (probably never sent to Constantinople) to garbled reports of Byzantine icon-worship at the Second Council of Nicaea
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Concept: this script, developed at centers like Corbie Abbey to standardize the Latin used in the Vulgate, became the basis of modern lowercase letters and remained dominant until about 1200
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this octagonal chapel at Aachen combines later barrel and groin vaults with an earlier design centering on an octagonal dome
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this chapel's architect, Odo of Metz, copied the layout of Ravenna's Basilica of San Vitale
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: this chapel features a pair of bronze doors nicknamed the "Wolf's Doors"
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Concept: this art style, named for Charlemagne, introduced the use of "westworks"
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Work: these illuminated Gospels were produced during Charlemagne's reign as part of the Carolingian Renaissance
work: The Carolingian Renaissance & the Court at Aachen

front: Figure: this Byzantine emperor partially recognized Charlemagne's imperial title as part of the Pax Nicephori, in exchange for lands in Dalmatia and the vassalage of Duke Borna
work: Diplomacy: Byzantium and the Abbasid Caliphate

front: Figure: this Byzantine empress first negotiated a marriage between her son Constantine VI and Charlemagne's daughter Rotrud
work: Diplomacy: Byzantium and the Abbasid Caliphate

front: Figure: this Byzantine empress reportedly negotiated to marry Charlemagne herself, shortly before his coronation
work: Diplomacy: Byzantium and the Abbasid Caliphate

front: Event: Abbasid Caliph Harun al-Rashid gifted Charlemagne this elephant, whose death (coinciding with that of Charlemagne's daughter) devastated him per Einhard
work: Diplomacy: Byzantium and the Abbasid Caliphate

front: Figure: Jewish Radhanite trader who served as translator on an embassy between Charlemagne and Harun al-Rashid
work: Diplomacy: Byzantium and the Abbasid Caliphate

front: Figure: Charlemagne's brother, with whom he briefly shared power; his death in 771 short-circuited a budding civil war between them
work: Family

front: Figure: Charlemagne's mother arranged his marriage to Desiderata of Lombardy
work: Family

front: Figure: Charlemagne's mother negotiated peace between him and his brother Carloman
work: Family

front: Figure: Charlemagne's illegitimate son had a hunched back from kyphosis
work: Family

front: Figure: this son of Charlemagne was disinherited after plotting against his father
work: Family

front: Figure: this son of Charlemagne died a monk at the abbey of Prum
work: Family

front: Figure: as chief royal scholar, this Englishman oversaw the schooling of both Pepin the Hunchback and Louis the Pious
work: Family

front: Figure: Charlemagne's grandson, who received Italy and other regions in the 843 Treaty of Verdun, which divided the empire among Louis the Pious's sons
work: Family

front: Figure: Charlemagne's nephew and greatest paladin, betrayed by Ganelon, who dies commanding the rearguard against Saracens at Roncevaux Pass
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Figure: this paladin blows the Oliphant horn to summon Charlemagne, who arrives too late, bursting his own temples in the process
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Figure: paladin who betrays Charlemagne's army to the Saracens, arranging the ambush at Roncevaux Pass; is torn apart by four horses as punishment after his champion Pinabel loses a trial by combat
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Work: in this poem, the 200-year-old Charlemagne wields the sword Joyeuse, which changes color thirty times a day
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Work: in this poem, Charlemagne wears his long white beard outside his armor, prompting his army to do likewise
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Work: in the poem's climactic battle, Charlemagne personally kills this Saracen emir
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Work: at the poem's end, Charlemagne is visited in his chamber by this angel and tearfully laments "God, how wearisome is my life!"
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Concept: the group of twelve elite warriors serving Charlemagne, including Roland, Archbishop Turpin, Oliver, Ogier the Dane, and Renaud
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Work: God commands Charlemagne to give this sword to his best warrior, Roland
work: Legacy: The Song of Roland

front: Work: in this story, an angel orders Charlemagne to commit theft, leading him to partner with the elfin bandit Elegast and confront his own treacherous brother-in-law Eggeric van Eggermonde
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Work: in this story, Charlemagne gives the magical size-shifting horse Bayard to Renaud
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Work: in this story, Renaud's brothers kill Charlemagne's nephew with a chessboard, then flee on the horse Bayard, whom Charlemagne later tries and fails to drown
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Figure: magical horse who can adjust his size to match his riders; given by Charlemagne to Renaud, later ridden in flight by Renaud's brothers after they kill Charlemagne's nephew
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Work: in this poem, Charlemagne and his Paladins travel to the rotating palace of Byzantine Emperor Hugo, held up by living bronze statues, after his wife claims Hugo is more handsome
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Work: this forged chronicle, purportedly written by Charlemagne's paladin Archbishop Turpin, was likely composed to promote pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Work: this chronicle recounts Charlemagne's forces battling the giant Ferracutus
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Figure: paladin who wields the sword Cortana and originally fights against Charlemagne before joining his court
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Figure: this paladin later departs for Avalon with the fairy Morgana
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Figure: fairy king who helps Huon of Bordeaux atone for accidentally killing Charlemagne's son
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Figure: monk (the "Monk of St. Gall") who collected anecdotes about Charlemagne
work: Legacy: Other Chansons de Geste and the Matter of France

front: Figure: this Belgian historian argued in Mohammed and Charlemagne that Charlemagne's rise would be inconceivable without the rise of Islam
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography

front: Figure: this historian's thesis holds that Arab conquests cutting off Mediterranean trade shifted economic power north to the Franks, enabling Charlemagne's rule
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography

front: Work: in this Ingres painting, Napoleon holds a scepter topped with a statuette of Charlemagne
back: Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne (Ingres)
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography

front: Work: in this Ingres painting, Napoleon's pose is modeled on the ancient Statue of Zeus at Olympia
back: Napoleon I on His Imperial Throne (Ingres)
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography

front: Work: this collection devotes its final third to legends of Charlemagne and his knights, alongside its sections on classical and Arthurian myth
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography

front: Work: this novel by Eyvind Johnson portrays Charlemagne presiding over a royal school at Aquisgranum (Aachen)
back: The Days of His Grace (Eyvind Johnson)
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography

front: Event: this World War II Waffen-SS division of French collaborators, formed from fleeing Milice members, was named for Charlemagne to symbolize a united Franco-German state
work: Legacy: Modern Memory & Historiography
